text: In this video, we present robotic complete mesocolic excision with high vascular tie of the iliocolic, right colic, and middle colic artery with intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis with omental pedicle flap. A seventy-four-year-old male patient diagnosed with ascending colon cancer previously had bilateral inguinal hernia repair, no distant metastasis identified preoperatively. He has significant comorbidities including obstructive sleep apnea requiring continuous positive airway pressure, hypopituitarism, pseudogood of right knee.

The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position following endotracheal intubation. The surgical team uses four 8mm robotic ports, one 8mm air seal port, and one 5mm assistant port. Robotic scissors, grasper, and bipolar instruments facilitate the dissection of the ileocolic vessels, proceeding from caudal to cranial and medial to lateral. The ileocolic pedicle is traced to the level of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), where the vein is identified and ligated, followed by the artery.
The surgeon identifies the midcolic pedicle and ligates the right-sided branches. The loop of Henle is then dissected and clipped.

The surgeon performs dissection from right to left, separating the omentum from the colon. Using the Firefly imaging system with indocyanine green, the transection site is identified. Robotic scissors are replaced by a robotic stapler to transect the colon. The separated colon specimen is placed atop the liver. An isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis is created using an additional stapler, and the two bowel segments are fixed with 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon creates an anisoperistaltic cytoside anastomosis using a staple, followed by making common enterotomies on the colon and small bowel with scissors. The left upper quadrant trocar is downsized from twelve millimeters to eight millimeters. The enterotomy site is closed with a running suture using 3-0 Vicryl, supplemented by additional interrupted sutures.

The surgeon prepares the omental pedicle graft and wraps it around the anastomosis site, ensuring final hemostasis. A 12-millimeter suprapubic trocar is inserted, and the specimen is extracted from the abdominal cavity.
